System Design

Core Stack

Section titled “Core Stack”

client -> transport -> TCP/TLS -> transport -> server -> engine

That line is the main architectural rule.

Repository Layout

Section titled “Repository Layout”

The workspace is split by responsibility. The tree below shows the main crates and the files that anchor each subsystem.

  • Cargo.toml workspace manifest and shared dependency graph

  • README.md project-level usage and development guidance

  • LLM.md canonical non-sensitive engineering context in the main repository

  • Directory

    .github

    • Directoryworkflows/
      • …
  • Directory

    crates

    • Directory

      command local command language and parsing

      • Cargo.toml

      • Directory

        src

        • lib.rs

        • command.rs command definitions and variants

        • lexer.rs tokenization for the REPL command language

        • parser.rs option-aware command parsing

        • error.rs command parse and validation errors

    • Directory

      transport shared wire protocol and framed I/O

      • Cargo.toml

      • Directory

        src

        • lib.rs

        • frame.rs binary frame layout

        • codec.rs request and response encoding and decoding

        • opcode.rs protocol operation identifiers

        • request.rs wire request structures

        • response.rs wire response structures

        • options.rs transport runtime options such as compression settings

        • error.rs transport-specific error surface

        • constants.rs protocol constants and limits

    • Directory

      engine state, durability, and recovery

      • Cargo.toml

      • Directory

        src

        • lib.rs

        • config.rs engine-level runtime and durability options

        • paths.rs on-disk path layout helpers

        • error.rs engine error types

        • Directory

          engine

          • … core state and execution internals
        • Directory

          store

          • … WAL, snapshots, manifest, crypto, and persistence helpers
    • Directory

      server network runtime and request orchestration

      • Cargo.toml

      • Directory

        src

        • main.rs server entrypoint

        • lib.rs

        • server.rs Tokio listener, sessions, and request flow

        • auth.rs mandatory authentication logic

        • tls.rs TLS acceptor and certificate wiring

        • metrics.rs runtime metrics collection

        • audit.rs append-only audit logging

        • args.rs CLI argument parsing

        • error.rs server error types

      • Directory

        tests

        • tcp_integration.rs network-level integration coverage
    • Directory

      client REPL and operator-facing UX

      • Cargo.toml

      • Directory

        src

        • main.rs client entrypoint

        • client.rs interactive request loop and rendering

        • args.rs URL and flag parsing

        • helper.rs REPL help text and command support

        • paths.rs local client path helpers

        • error.rs client-side error types

Crate Responsibilities

Section titled “Crate Responsibilities”

command

Section titled “command”
  • command definitions
  • lexer
  • parser
  • command metadata

transport

Section titled “transport”
  • frame format
  • request and response encoding
  • opcodes
  • checksums
  • compression flags
  • sync and async I/O helpers

engine

Section titled “engine”
  • key/value state
  • expirations
  • WAL
  • snapshots
  • manifest
  • recovery
  • storage encryption

server

Section titled “server”
  • Tokio networking
  • TLS accept
  • authentication
  • per-session lifecycle
  • runtime guardrails
  • audit logging
  • engine runtime worker

client

Section titled “client”
  • REPL
  • connection strings
  • TLS client setup
  • output rendering

How the Pieces Connect

Section titled “How the Pieces Connect”

The repository structure mirrors the runtime layering directly:

  • client turns user input into command values and sends them through transport
  • transport owns framing, opcodes, checksums, and message encoding for both sides
  • server accepts TCP or TLS connections, authenticates sessions, enforces guardrails, and routes decoded requests
  • engine applies state changes, maintains durability, and recovers from persisted storage

That separation is the reason transport changes do not require socket parsing inside the engine, and engine changes do not require protocol logic inside storage code.

Why the Engine Worker Exists

Section titled “Why the Engine Worker Exists”

The current server accepts multiple client connections concurrently, but mutating engine work is still serialized through a dedicated engine worker. That gives:

  • deterministic single-node ordering
  • a clean place for atomic transaction application
  • less shared-state complexity in the async server path

It is a deliberate single-node design, not a distributed concurrency model.
